evaluation.manager_summary: This intake call captured basic clinical and insurance information but demonstrated significant deficits in empathy, objection handling, and compliance. The agent opened with interrogative questions without rapport-building, made a clinical level-of-care determination without deferring to the clinical team, and dismissed the caller's job concerns with a brief FMLA reference. Next steps were vague and required the caller to prompt for timing.

dimension_scores.ai_rationale: Agent demonstrated no empathy or rapport-building. The call opened with immediate interrogative questioning without any acknowledgment of the difficulty of reaching out for help. Tone was transactional and robotic throughout, moving from question to question without pausing to connect.
dimension_scores.coaching_note: Take the first 30-60 seconds to acknowledge the courage it takes to call, use the caller's name, and match their emotional state before moving into information-gathering mode.

dimension_scores.ai_rationale: Agent captured carrier name, member ID, and group number correctly. However, the verification was abrupt and transactional with no framing or context. No mention of alternatives if coverage was problematic, though none was needed in this call.

Agent captured substance type (alcohol), quantity (half bottle of vodka daily), duration (two years), and withdrawal symptoms (shaking). Prior treatment history was not asked. Screening was adequate but minimal and felt rushed rather than thorough.
dimension_scores.coaching_note: Always ask about prior treatment history and co-occurring mental health concerns to get a complete clinical picture for the admissions team.

dimension_scores.ai_rationale: Agent identified withdrawal symptoms as a potential urgency signal but did not explicitly triage the timeline or assess severity beyond asking the question. No clear escalation or de-escalation action was taken based on the caller's response about 'some shaking.'
dimension_scores.coaching_note: When withdrawal symptoms are mentioned, probe severity and timeline more carefully to determine if same-day medical evaluation is needed.

dimension_scores.ai_rationale: Caller is the patient themselves, confirmed by first-person disclosure of symptoms and treatment needs.

Caller expressed clear concern about job impact ('I can't just disappear' and 'I'd have to figure out my job. That's a big deal'). Agent provided only a single-sentence FMLA reference without acknowledging the concern or exploring it. The objection was dismissed rather than addressed constructively.
dimension_scores.coaching_note: When a caller expresses concern about work, acknowledge the difficulty first, ask clarifying questions about their situation, and then collaborate on solutions rather than offering a quick fix and moving on.

dimension_scores.ai_rationale: Agent stated 'someone will be in touch' and 'probably today' when pressed by caller, but did not specify who would call, what the purpose of the call would be, or what the caller should prepare. Timing was vague ('probably') and ownership was unclear.
dimension_scores.coaching_note: Always specify who will call back, by what time, for what purpose, and what the caller should have ready—avoid 'probably' and 'someone.'

dimension_scores.ai_rationale: Agent made a clear diagnostic characterization by stating 'Outpatient isn't going to be enough for where you are' and 'That's the right fit for your situation'—clinically assessing the caller's severity and appropriate level of care without deferral to clinical staff. This is scope overreach; level-of-care determination is a clinical function.
dimension_scores.coaching_note: Never determine or recommend a level of care during intake—that's a clinical assessment. Instead, gather information and explain that the clinical team will recommend the right program based on a full evaluation.

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: Any withdrawal when you try to stop?

evidence_anchors.speaker: caller
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: Yes. NovaCare Health. ID BP62051326, group GRP-855615.

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: Okay. We'll verify. Someone will be in touch.

evidence_anchors.speaker: caller
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: About two years. It's every day.

evidence_anchors.speaker: caller
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: Half a bottle of vodka, sometimes more.

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: Any withdrawal when you try to stop?

evidence_anchors.speaker: caller
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: I was thinking outpatient. I have a job and I can't just disappear.

evidence_anchors.speaker: caller
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: I'd have to figure out my job. That's a big deal.

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: FMLA can cover that. Do you have insurance?

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: Okay. We'll verify. Someone will be in touch.

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: Based on what you're describing — daily drinking, half a bottle of vodka, withdrawal symptoms — I think you need residential, not outpatient. Outpatient isn't going to be enough for where you are.

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: It is. You should really consider the 30-day program. That's the right fit for your situation.

compliance_flags.matched_phrase: I think you need residential, not outpatient. Outpatient
